destination_overview.best_time_to_visit: Your dates (April 1–10) are the absolute peak for the 'Somei Yoshino' Sakura bloom in Tokyo. This is the most prestigious and beautiful time to visit, coinciding with major spring festivals.

recommended_places.name: Kodokan Judo Institute
recommended_places.description: The headquarters of the worldwide Judo community, founded by Kano Jigoro. It features 8 massive dojos, including the main hall with 420 mats.
recommended_places.why_recommended: This is the 'Mecca' of Judo. As you requested the biggest training halls, this is the most significant site globally.
tips: Visitors can watch practice from the 8th-floor spectator gallery.
Check their calendar for high-ranking dan grading events.
recommended_places.best_time_to_visit: Late afternoon to see active training.

recommended_places.name: Nippon Budokan
recommended_places.description: An indoor arena originally built for the 1964 Olympics judo competition. It is the spiritual home of Japanese martial arts.
recommended_places.why_recommended: It is an architectural icon and the primary venue for major martial arts championships.
tips: It's located inside Kitanomaru Park, which is also a top Sakura spot.
The octagonal roof is designed to resemble Mt. Fuji.

recommended_places.name: Chidorigafuchi Moat
recommended_places.description: One of Tokyo's most famous cherry blossom spots where hundreds of trees line the Imperial Palace moats.
recommended_places.why_recommended: The most picturesque Sakura spot in Tokyo. Renting a boat here is a quintessential luxury experience during your dates.
tips: Expect long lines for boats; arrive 30 mins before opening.
The night illumination is spectacular.

recommended_places.name: Mount Takao (Takao-san)
recommended_places.description: A sacred mountain offering beautiful hiking trails, a temple, and views of Mt. Fuji on clear days.
recommended_places.why_recommended: Matches your request for a mountain activity near Tokyo. It offers a blend of nature and Shinto culture.
tips: Take the cable car to the halfway point to save time.
Eat 'Soba' noodles at the base; it's a local specialty.

recommended_places.name: Shinjuku Gyoen National Garden
recommended_places.description: A massive park featuring Japanese, English, and French gardens with over 1,000 cherry trees.
recommended_places.why_recommended: Perfect for a luxury picnic under the Sakura. It's spacious and offers a more relaxed pace than other spots.
tips: Alcohol is prohibited, keeping the vibe peaceful and sophisticated.
Advance reservations are often required during Sakura peak.

recommended_places.name: Narizawa
recommended_places.description: A world-renowned 2-Michelin-starred restaurant focusing on 'Satoyama' cuisine (nature-to-plate).
recommended_places.why_recommended: Fits your luxury travel style and interest in top-tier Japanese meals. It's a culinary journey through Japan's forests.
tips: Book at least 2 months in advance.
The 'Bread of the Forest' dish is a must-see for photography.

recommended_places.name: Aman Spa Tokyo
recommended_places.description: The ultimate luxury wellness sanctuary in Tokyo, featuring a 30-meter pool overlooking the city.
recommended_places.why_recommended: High-end relaxation for User 1. It provides a 'beach-like' serenity with its massive pool and panoramic views.
tips: Try the 'Misogi' purification ritual.
Non-hotel guests can book spa treatments to access the facilities.

recommended_places.name: Meguro River Cherry Blossoms
recommended_places.description: Nearly 800 cherry trees line the river, forming a pink tunnel over the water.
recommended_places.why_recommended: The most 'Instagrammable' spot for User 2. The pink lanterns and champagne stalls create a festive luxury atmosphere.
tips: Visit the Starbucks Reserve Roastery nearby for a luxury coffee experience with a view.
Go at night to see the lanterns lit up.

recommended_places.name: Sukiyabashi Jiro Roppongi Hills
recommended_places.description: Managed by the son of Jiro Ono, this sushi destination offers world-class Edomae sushi in a more accessible setting than the Ginza original.
recommended_places.why_recommended: Essential Japanese meal experience for high-budget travelers.
tips: Reservations are mandatory and can be made through luxury hotel concierges.
Dress code is smart casual.

recommended_places.name: TeamLab Borderless (Azabudai Hills)
recommended_places.description: A world of artworks without boundaries, a museum where art moves and interacts with other works.
recommended_places.why_recommended: Perfect for high-quality content creation and a modern cultural experience.
tips: Wear white clothing for the best photos as the light projects onto you.
Buy tickets weeks in advance.

recommended_places.name: Hoshinoya Tokyo Onsen
recommended_places.description: A luxury Ryokan in the heart of the city with a rooftop open-air hot spring bath fed by waters from 1,500m below ground.
recommended_places.why_recommended: Offers traditional Japanese wellness in a hyper-luxury setting.
tips: The bath is open to the sky, allowing you to watch the clouds while soaking.
Consider staying here for the full experience.

recommended_places.name: Shibuya Sky
recommended_places.description: A 229m tall open-air observation deck providing 360-degree views of Tokyo, including the Shibuya Crossing.
recommended_places.why_recommended: The most popular photography spot in Tokyo currently.
tips: Book the 'Paradise Lounge' for a luxury drink with a view.
The 'Sky Edge' corner is the best for photos.

recommended_places.name: Zaimokuza Beach (Kamakura)
recommended_places.description: A long sandy beach near the historic city of Kamakura, perfect for a luxury day trip from Tokyo.
recommended_places.why_recommended: Since User 1 wants 'Beach Time', this is the most sophisticated coastal area within reach of Tokyo.
tips: Visit 'Zaimokuza Terrace' for high-end dining by the sea.
Combine with a visit to the Great Buddha of Kamakura.

recommended_places.name: Ginza Kojyu
recommended_places.description: A 3-Michelin-starred Kaiseki restaurant that serves seasonal masterpieces.
recommended_places.why_recommended: The pinnacle of Japanese 'Kaiseki' dining, perfect for your luxury budget.
tips: Ask to sit at the counter to watch the chef work.
Very strict punctuality is expected.

recommended_places.name: Senso-ji Temple
recommended_places.description: Tokyo's oldest temple, famous for its massive red lantern and vibrant Nakamise shopping street.
recommended_places.why_recommended: The center of Japanese tradition in Tokyo. Great for cultural immersion.
tips: Rent a high-end Kimono for a photo shoot here.
Explore the side streets for high-quality Japanese knives and crafts.
recommended_places.best_time_to_visit: Early morning (07:00) to avoid huge crowds.

recommended_places.name: Hakone Private Onsen (Gora Kadan)
recommended_places.description: Located in a mountain setting, this is one of Japan's most exclusive Ryokans, formerly a retreat for the Imperial family.
recommended_places.why_recommended: Combines 'Going to Mountain' with 'Luxury Wellness'. It's the ultimate relaxation experience.
tips: Book a 'Day Plan' if not staying overnight.
The train journey via the 'Romancecar' from Shinjuku is part of the experience.
recommended_places.best_time_to_visit: Spring for Sakura in the mountains.

recommended_places.name: Meiji Jingu Shrine
recommended_places.description: A Shinto shrine dedicated to Emperor Meiji, surrounded by a lush forest of 100,000 trees.
recommended_places.why_recommended: Offers a meditative escape in the center of Tokyo. High cultural value.
tips: Look for traditional wedding processions on weekends.
Visit the Inner Garden (Gyoen) for the famous iris pond.

recommended_places.name: Imperial Palace Private Guided Tour
recommended_places.description: The primary residence of the Emperor of Japan, built on the site of the old Edo Castle.
recommended_places.why_recommended: A luxury private tour allows for deeper historical understanding and better photo access.
tips: The East Garden is free and excellent for Sakura.
Book official tours through the Imperial Household Agency website.

recommended_places.name: Suntory Hall
recommended_places.description: Tokyo's premier concert hall for classical music, known for its superb acoustics.
recommended_places.why_recommended: A sophisticated evening activity matching the luxury lifestyle and interest in events.
tips: Check for the Spring Festival schedule.
Formal attire is recommended.

recommended_places.name: Omotesando Hills
recommended_places.description: A high-end shopping complex designed by Tadao Ando, representing the 'Champs-Élysées' of Tokyo.
recommended_places.why_recommended: The center of luxury fashion and architectural photography in Tokyo.
tips: The ramped interior is a marvel of design.
Visit the many high-end cafes in the backstreets (Ura-Hara).

hidden_gems.name: Nezu Museum Garden
hidden_gems.why_special: Private luxury garden with ancient tea houses and Sakura.

practical_information.getting_around: With your budget, use a private chauffeur service for city travel or the 'Gran Class' on Shinkansen for day trips. Taxis are also very reliable but expensive.
practical_information.local_currency: Japanese Yen (JPY). Though card acceptance is growing, carry cash for shrines and traditional shops.
language_tips: Ichi-go ichi-e (One time, one meeting - Cherish the moment)
Sakura wa kirei desu ne (The cherry blossoms are beautiful, aren't they?)
cultural_notes: In dojos (like Kodokan), always bow when entering or leaving the mat area.
Sakura viewing (Hanami) is often accompanied by 'O-bentos' (luxury lunch boxes). Buy these at Takashimaya or Mitsukoshi Depachikas.
